Optimal wavelength considering Magnesium's optical properties

Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of magnesium alloys to avoid melting (low melting point ~650°C); starting at 200W balances efficiency and safety. Max limited to prevent substrate damage due to high reflectivity at 1064 nm. Source: Studies on Mg alloy cleaning (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018, Vol. 30, IPG Photonics specs for Yb-fiber lasers).

Effective speeds for contamination removal; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy rust/oils, faster (2000-3000 mm/s) for light contaminants. Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance on Mg to minimize heat accumulation. Source: Industry standards from Laserax and Adapt Laser manuals for metal surface prep.

Nanosecond regime preferred for Mg to ablate contaminants with minimal HAZ; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces thermal effects but increases cost. 50 ns recommended for low reflectivity materials like Mg. Source: Laser physics research (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020, Vol. 132, on pulse duration effects in metal cleaning).

Typical for pulsed fiber lasers in cleaning; higher rates speed up process but risk heat buildup on thermally conductive Mg. 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Trade-off: >80 kHz may cause melting. Source: Academic literature (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019, Vol. 475, on rep rate optimization for light metals).

Fluence below Mg damage threshold (~5 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe cleaning; 0.5-2 J/cm² for light contaminants, up to 4 for heavy. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² avoids ablation of substrate. Source: Ablation threshold studies (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2017, Vol. 50, on IR laser interaction with Mg).

Smaller spots (100-200 μm) for fine detail on Mg surfaces, larger (300-500 μm) for broad area prep. 200 μm recommended for uniform cleaning without excessive heat concentration. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems for metals.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers on Mg to prevent overheating per pass. Recommended 2 passes for industrial removal. Source: Industry case studies (e.g., SPIE Proc. 2021, Vol. 11660, on multi-pass cleaning of alloys).

Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or overexposure; 30-50% for large areas, 60-80% for detailed Mg cleaning. 60% recommended to balance efficiency and avoid thermal damage. Source: Process optimization studies (Opt. Laser Technol. 2022, Vol. 145, on scan overlap in laser cleaning).
